## Scope (edit ONLY this chapter)
`blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`, the block
`\begin{lemma}\label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}` (the single Mathlib-absent brick the
iter-235 reframe reduced the affine lane to).

## Problem to fix (blueprint-reviewer ts236 must-fix/soon #4)
The brick block currently has NO `\lean{...}` hint and NO `\begin{proof}` body — only a
remark stating what it discharges. The next prover (mathlib-build) has no target name and
no proof strategy. Supply both.

## Required additions

### 1. `\lean{...}` hint
Add `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}` to the lemma block.
(Tag the name as the expected target; the prover may adjust the exact namespace, but this
is the intended declaration.)

### 2. Statement (restate cleanly if needed, project notation)
For a ring map `φ : R → R'` (`CommRingCat`), with `M` an `R'`-module (so `tilde M` is the
associated quasi-coherent module on `Spec R'`), there is a canonical isomorphism of
`(Spec R).Modules`
\[
  (\mathrm{pushforward}\,(\mathrm{Spec.map}\,\varphi))\,(\widetilde{M})
    \;\cong\; \widetilde{\,\mathrm{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\;M\,}.
\]

### 3. `\begin{proof}` sketch (mathematical prose; NO Lean tactic strings)
This is a project-bespoke Mathlib-gradient lemma (no external source line needed). Convey
the following construction as textbook prose:

- **Sections agree on `⊤` with no transport.** `Spec.map φ` pulls back the top open to the
  top open (`(Spec.map φ)⁻¹ ⊤ = ⊤` holds definitionally), and the pushforward of a module
  evaluated on `⊤` is the module evaluated on the preimage of `⊤`. Hence the global
  sections of the left side are `M` (as the global sections of `tilde M`), and the global
  sections of the right side are `restrictScalars φ M` — the SAME underlying abelian group,
  now viewed as an `R`-module.

- **The comparison ring map is `φ` itself.** The structure-sheaf comparison map at `⊤`
  underlying `Spec.map φ` is the global-sections map `Γ(Spec R, ⊤) ⟶ Γ(Spec R', ⊤)`, which
  is canonically conjugate to `φ` by the naturality of the `Γ ⊣ Spec` adjunction unit/counit
  iso (`Scheme.ΓSpecIso` naturality). So the `R`-action on the pushforward sections is
  exactly restriction of scalars along `φ`.

- **Assemble the iso via `tilde` full-faithfulness / the `Γ`–`tilde` adjunction.** `tilde`
  is fully faithful with essential image the quasi-coherent modules, and the counit
  `fromTildeΓ` is an isomorphism on quasi-coherent objects. Applying `tilde` to the
  global-sections identification (`M` over `R'` ↦ `restrictScalars φ M` over `R`) and
  composing with the counit isomorphisms on both sides yields the asserted object iso.

- **The one residual scalar-compatibility check** (if it surfaces at the `ModuleCat` level)
  is the standard restriction-of-scalars compatibility: materialise the intermediate action
  with the composite-homomorphism module structure and the scalar-tower compatibility along
  `algebraMap`, exactly the idiom Mathlib's own `Tilde` construction uses to discharge the
  same `map_smul`-shaped goal. (Name the mathematical fact — restriction of scalars commutes
  with the `tilde` functor on global sections via the `algebraMap` scalar tower — not a Lean
  tactic block.)

### 4. Add a one-line corollary remark
Note that this single object iso also discharges, as immediate corollaries: (a) the
quasi-coherence of `pushforward (Spec.map φ)(tilde M)` (the QC class is closed under
isomorphism and `tilde N` is quasi-coherent), and (b) the Γ-fragment iso needed by
`affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (apply global sections to the object iso). So it is the
sole remaining gap for the whole affine lane.

## Constraints
- Mathematical prose only; no Lean tactic strings as content (Lean identifier names may be
  mentioned in passing as the name of the cited Mathlib fact).
- Do NOT add/remove `\leanok`/`\mathlibok` markers.
- Keep the existing tilde-full-faithfulness reframe and the `[IsQuasicoherent F]`-based
  statements of the two theorems intact; you are only filling the brick block.

## Out of scope
Do not touch the locality lemmas, the two main theorems' statements, or any other chapter.

content:
1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	\leanok
96	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
97	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
98	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
99	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
100	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
101	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
102	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
103	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
104	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
105	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
107	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
108	\end{proof}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}\leanok
111	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
112	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
114	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
115	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
116	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
117	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
118	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
119	  isomorphism.
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
124	  \leanok
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
138	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
139	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
141	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
143	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
144	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
149	  \leanok
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	\begin{lemma}
166	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
167	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
168	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
169	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
170	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
171	  \[
172	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
173	    \;\cong\;
174	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
175	  \]
176	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
177	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
178	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
179	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
180	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
181	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
182	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
183	
184	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
185	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
186	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
187	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
188	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
189	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
190	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
191	\end{lemma}
192	
193	\begin{remark}
194	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
195	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
196	  \begin{enumerate}
197	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
198	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
199	      \(R\)-module of sections of
200	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
201	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
202	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
203	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
204	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
205	      closed under isomorphism; hence
206	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
207	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
208	      needed for the affine lane.
209	  \end{enumerate}
210	\end{remark}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}\leanok
213	[Affine base change]
214	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
215	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
216	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
217	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
218	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
219	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
220	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
221	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
222	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
223	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
224	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
225	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
226	  % we have
227	  % $$
228	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
229	  % $$"
230	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
231	  change''.}
232	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
233	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
234	  above the canonical map of
235	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
236	  \[
237	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
238	  \]
239	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
240	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
241	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
242	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
243	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
244	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
245	  fails.
246	\end{lemma}
247	
248	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
249	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
250	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
251	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
252	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
253	% for some $A$-module $M$.
254	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
255	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
256	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
257	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
258	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
259	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
260	% equality
261	% $$
262	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
263	% $$
264	% as $R'$-modules."
265	% NOTE: Lean proof in progress. The locality reduction
266	% (lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens) lands; the affine computation is reframed
267	% around full-faithfulness of the tilde functor (essential image = the
268	% quasi-coherent modules), which collapses "iso of Spec-modules" to "iso of a
269	% concrete ModuleCat R' map" and avoids all section-level SMul/Module-instance
270	% plumbing. The single Mathlib-absent ingredient is
271	% lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso (next FlatBaseChange prover target); the closing
272	% algebra is TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange, present in
273	% Mathlib with no flatness. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
274	\begin{proof}
275	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
276	  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
277	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
278	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
279	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine
280	  open. The assertion is then local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all
281	  schemes in sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
282	  \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\)
283	  corresponding to a ring map \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the
284	  quasi-coherent sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that
285	  quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used: it is what licenses writing
286	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) over the affine open.) Computing the fibre product
287	  gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
288	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
289	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). In this affine
290	  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
291	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
292	
293	  \medskip
294	  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
295	  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
296	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
297	  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
298	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
299	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
300	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
301	  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
302	  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
303	  \[
304	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
305	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
306	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
307	  \]
308	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
309	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
310	  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
311	  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
312	  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
313	  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
314	  conjugate to \(\varphi\) via the naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\operatorname{Spec}\)
315	  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
316	  again the top open, so the identification of sections needs no transport. Any
317	  residual scalar identity in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) is settled by the
318	  scalar-tower compatibility of restriction of scalars.
319	
320	  \medskip
321	  \emph{Identification of the concrete map.} Under
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, pushforward along the affine morphism
323	  is restriction of scalars on modules and pullback along \(g\) is
324	  \(- \otimes_R R'\); accordingly \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is identified with the
325	  canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison map between the two ways of forming the
326	  iterated tensor product. The claim therefore reduces to the associativity /
327	  cancellation isomorphism
328	  \[
329	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\cong\; R' \otimes_R M
330	  \]
331	  of \(R'\)-modules, which is an isomorphism (no flatness is needed in the affine
332	  case).
333	\end{proof}
334	
335	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
336	
337	\begin{theorem}\leanok
338	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
339	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
341	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
342	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
343	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
344	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
345	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
346	  % $$
347	  % \xymatrix{
348	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
349	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
350	  % }
351	  % $$
352	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
353	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
354	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
355	  % For any $i \geq 0$
356	  % \begin{enumerate}
357	  % \item the base change map of
358	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
359	  % is an isomorphism
360	  % $$
361	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
362	  % $$
363	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
364	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
365	  % \end{enumerate}"
366	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
367	  \(i = 0\) case.}
368	  Consider the cartesian square
369	  \[
370	    \begin{array}{ccc}
371	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
372	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
373	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
374	    \end{array}
375	  \]
376	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
377	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
378	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
379	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
380	  \[
381	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
382	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
383	  \]
384	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
385	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
386	  \[
387	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
388	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
389	  \]
390	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
391	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
392	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
393	
394	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
395	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
396	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
397	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
398	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
399	  an isomorphism.
400	\end{theorem}
401	
402	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
403	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
404	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
405	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
406	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
407	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
408	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
409	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
410	% (equality by
411	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
412	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
413	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
414	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
415	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
416	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
417	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
418	%
419	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
420	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
421	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
422	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
423	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
424	% We are trying to show that the map
425	% $$
426	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
427	% $$
428	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
429	% is an isomorphism.
430	%
431	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
432	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
433	% $$
434	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
435	% $$
436	% see
437	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
438	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
439	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
440	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
441	% $$
442	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
443	% $$
444	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
445	% $$
446	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
447	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
448	% $$
449	% as follows from
450	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
451	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
452	% NOTE: Lean proof is a documented sorry (deferred). Its affine input is the
453	% affine lemma above, which in turn reduces to lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso (the
454	% affine-pushforward-of-tilde brick) plus the tilde full-faithfulness reframe. On
455	% top of that it needs Čech-cohomology / affine-cover infrastructure for
456	% SheafOfModules, which Mathlib does not provide. This remains a multi-lane engine
457	% build, not a single-iter target. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
460	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
461	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
462	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
463	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
464	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
465	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
466	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
467	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
468	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
469	  \(B\)-modules
470	  \[
471	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
472	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
473	  \]
474	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
475	
476	  \medskip
477	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
478	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
479	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
480	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
481	  computes sheaf cohomology:
482	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
483	  the cover yields an affine open cover
484	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
485	  \(X_B\), with the same property
486	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
487	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
488	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
489	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
490	  \[
491	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
492	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
493	  \]
494	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
495	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
496	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
497	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
498	
499	  \medskip
500	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
501	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
502	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
503	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
504	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
505	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
506	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
507	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
508	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
509	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
510	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
511	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
512	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
513	  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
514	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
515	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
516	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
517	  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
518	\end{proof}
519

input.new_string:
identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \emph{Sections agree on the top open with no transport.} The morphism
  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) pulls the top open of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)
  back to the top open of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\): the preimage
  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top)\) is \(\top\) definitionally. By
  definition the pushforward of a module evaluated on an open \(U\) is the module
  evaluated on the preimage of \(U\); hence on \(\top\) the global sections of the
  left-hand side \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) are the
  global sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(\top\), namely the underlying abelian
  group of \(M\). The global sections of the right-hand side
  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) are the underlying abelian group
  of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\), which is the \emph{same} abelian group,
  now regarded as an \(R\)-module. So at the level of underlying abelian groups the
  two sides carry identical global sections and no transport along the open-set
  identification is needed.

  \medskip
  \emph{The comparison ring map is \(\varphi\) itself.} The structure-sheaf
  comparison map at \(\top\) underlying \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is the
  global-sections map
  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\).
  Under the natural isomorphism \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec}(-)) \cong (-)\)
  furnished by the \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction
  (the naturality of the iso \(\mathrm{Scheme.}\Gamma\mathrm{SpecIso}\)), this
  global-sections map is canonically conjugate to \(\varphi : R \to R'\) itself.
  Consequently the \(R\)-module structure that the pushforward puts on its sections
  is exactly the \(R\)-action obtained from the \(R'\)-action on \(M\) by
  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\); that is, the section-level module is
  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) on the nose.

  \medskip
  \emph{Assemble the object isomorphism via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
  functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful with essential image the
  quasi-coherent modules, and the counit comparison
  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism on every
  quasi-coherent object \(\mathcal{N}\) (this is the content of the counit
  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\) being invertible on quasi-coherent modules). Both
  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) and
  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) are quasi-coherent. Applying the
  functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) to the global-sections identification of the previous
  two paragraphs — which sends \(M\) over \(R'\) to \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\)
  over \(R\) — produces an isomorphism between the tildes of the two global-section
  modules, and composing with the counit isomorphisms on each side transports this
  into the asserted isomorphism of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\).

  \medskip
  \emph{The residual scalar-compatibility check.} Should a residual identity
  surface at the \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) level — the assertion that the map
  respects scalar multiplication — it is the standard restriction-of-scalars
  compatibility: the intermediate action is materialised through the
  composite-homomorphism module structure, and the two scalar actions agree by the
  scalar-tower compatibility along \(\operatorname{algebraMap}\). This is precisely
  the mathematical fact that restriction of scalars commutes with the
  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) functor on global sections via the \(\operatorname{algebraMap}\)
  scalar tower — the same idiom Mathlib's own \(\mathrm{Tilde}\) construction uses
  to discharge the analogous \(\mathrm{map\_smul}\)-shaped goal.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}

# blueprint-writer — fbc-brick report (iter-236)

## Scope
Edited ONLY `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`, the
`\label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}` brick block. No other lemma, theorem, or
chapter touched.

## Changes made
1. **`\lean{...}` hint added.** `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}`
   placed on the lemma block (after the `\label`), giving the next prover a concrete
   target name.

2. **`\begin{proof}` sketch added** (textbook prose, no Lean tactic strings). Four
   movements, matching the directive:
   - Sections agree on `⊤` with no transport (`(Spec.map φ)⁻¹ ⊤ = ⊤` definitional;
     pushforward-on-`⊤` = module on preimage; same underlying abelian group `M`
     vs `restrictScalars φ M`).
   - The comparison ring map is `φ` itself, via naturality of the
     `Γ ⊣ Spec` adjunction iso (`Scheme.ΓSpecIso`) ⇒ the section `R`-action is
     restriction of scalars along `φ`.
   - Assemble the object iso via tilde full-faithfulness + counit (`fromTildeΓ`)
     isomorphisms on both (quasi-coherent) sides.
   - Residual `map_smul`-shaped scalar-compatibility = restriction-of-scalars
     commutes with `tilde` on global sections via the `algebraMap` scalar tower
     (named as the math fact, the idiom Mathlib's `Tilde` uses).
   Lean identifier names appear only as names of cited facts, never as tactic blocks.

3. **Corollary note added** to the existing remark: a closing sentence stating that,
   because both inputs the affine reduction needs (the Γ-fragment comparison feeding
   `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward) are
   corollaries of this one object iso, the lemma is the sole remaining Mathlib-absent
   gap for the whole affine lane. (The remark already enumerated (a) and (b); this
   ties them off.)

## Constraints honored
- No `\leanok`/`\mathlibok` markers added or removed (the brick remains unproven —
  no `\leanok` on lemma or proof).
- Tilde-full-faithfulness reframe and the `[IsQuasicoherent F]`-based theorem
  statements left intact.
- Mathematical prose only.

## Note for the next prover
The expected target namespace is `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`; the
prover may adjust the exact namespace. The closing algebra for the consuming affine
theorem (`TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange`) is already present in
Mathlib with no flatness — this brick is the last gap.
